fr:mainmatter.html:p: I'll pause here to introduce a useful idea: the idea of sentences being equivalent to each other in a structure. (Think "equi-valent" like "equal-valued".)
Sentences A and B are equivalent in a structure iff, for every valuation v in that structure, v(A) = v(B).

Note two things: first, we need to have a structure in mind to say when two sentences are equivalent, and we might get different results by looking at different structures. Second, once we have a structure in mind, equivalence looks at every valuation for that structure, at every possible way of assigning values to atomic formulas.

For example, p and 
eg 
eg p are equivalent in the Boolean structure: no matter what Boolean valuation v we look at, v(p) = v(
eg 
eg p). But p and q are not equivalent in the Boolean structure. Yes, there are some Boolean valuations that give the same value to p as to q, but that doesn't matter. Since there is at least one Boolean valuation w with w(p) 
eq w(q), that means that p and q are not equivalent in the Boolean structure.

We've set up our implication in the Dunn-Belnap structure so that p \rightarrow q comes out equivalent in this structure to \neg p \vee q. That was inspired by the Boolean structure, where these same two sentences are also equivalent. And it will continue into our next structure as well. However, this is not required; structures can set up their connectives any which way. If you want to play with some structures where p \rightarrow q and \neg p \vee q are not equivalent, you can check out some of the structures at https://negation.rocks/emu.html.

In the first week, we learned about the kinds of languages we're going to work with this semester. These languages contain sentences like p \land \neg (q \lor \bot ) and so on. At the moment, this is just an unintelligible string of symbols. This week we'll take our first steps into endowing our languages with meaning. For now, we won't worry about connecting arguments and logics to this work on meaning just yet; we'll bring it all together next week. This week, we're focusing just on languages. (Recall that our default language, the one to focus on for now, uses infinitely many atomic formulas, plus the connectives \land , \lor , \rightarrow , \neg , \bot with arities 2, 2, 2, 1, 0, respectively. If you have any question about what that all means, check back to lecture 1!)

The approach we're going to start with for bringing meaning to these sentences uses truth values. These start to give us a limited grip on something like meaning. Real sentences, in languages like English, are true or false, when they are, in part because of what they mean. The sentence "There are at least nine planets in our solar system" is false, in part because of what bodies are out there in space, but also in part because of a convention about the word "planet" adopted by the International Astronomical Union in 2006. (Before this convention was adopted, Pluto was also considered a planet, making the sentence true.) Of course, there's much more to the meaning of a real sentence than just whether it's true or false! But truth and falsity are at least wrapped up in the meaning of real sentences, so we're at least in the right area to be poking around.

fr:title.text: A first example: two truth values

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: A first example: two truth values

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Let's start out by sup posing that there are only two possibilities for a sentence: either it's true or else it's false. Furthermore, let's suppose that no sentence can ever be both true and false. (In a moment, we'll look at relaxing these assumptions, but it simplifies things to start out with them in place.) This gives us two truth values. I'll call them \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, but it doesn't really matter what we call them; we just need some names or other, and I'll use these.

We will connect our language to these truth values in two stages. The first stage involves just the atomic sentences of our language, and is characterised by total freedom. For each atomic sentence, we choose one of the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} for it to have. Any way of making such a choice for every atomic sentence is called a valuation. So, for example, there is a valuation that assigns the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} to every atomic sentence; and there is another valuation that assigns \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} to every atomic sentence. There is a valuation that assigns \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} just to p and r and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} to all the other atomic sentences. And there are very many other valuations besides.

Some notation: if we have a valuation named v, then we can talk about the value it assigns to an atomic sentence p by writing v(p). So if v assigns the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} to p, then we can record this more efficiently by saying that v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. And if a different valuation w assigns the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} to p, then we can record this by saying that w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}.

When we work with these valuations, we're again bumping into infinity here, in two ways. First, since we have infinitely many atomic sentences, each individual valuation is itself infinite; it must assign one or the other of \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} to every one of the infinitely many atomic sentences. For this reason, while it's possible to fully specify a few example valuations, as I did two paragraphs ago, there are a lot of valuations out there that can't be fully specified at all, since they don't fit any kind of neat pattern.

Second, not only is each valuation infinite, but there are infinitely many different valuations. We're going to handle both of these sources of infinity in our usual blase way. The second source we can ignore entirely; it just doesn't really matter for our purposes how many valuations there are. The fact that there are so staggeringly many may be interesting, but isn't actually going to affect us at all. The first, though, we'll need to make some provision for. For the most part, this will be pretty subtle, and you won't need to lose any sleep over it, but I'll point it out here.

In the course of an example, I might say something like "Take a valuation v where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}". What I want to note here is that this does not fully specify a valuation v. A valuation must assign some value to every atomic sentence, and we're supposing a language with infinitely many. Lots of different valuations will meet the above condition on v. What I'm saying when I put something forward like this is just to take any valuation that meets the specification. The fact that lots of different valuations meet the specification is fine.

So that's our first stage of connecting our language to the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}: we use valuations, which are ways of assigning these values to the atomic sentences of the language. Absolutely any such way of assigning counts as a valuation, so there are lots of different valuations. In our second stage, we move on to compound sentences, letting each valuation assign values to these as well. Here, though, in contrast with the atomic sentences, there is zero freedom. A valuation can assign any values at all to the atomic sentences, but once that assignment is made, that fully determines what value the valuation will assign to every compound sentence.

This difference between atomic and compound sentences---total freedom for atomics, and total constraint for compounds---is the key to how we're going to put these valuations to use. We need total freedom for the atomic sentences because, well, there's not much to them. There's nothing about p that means it should get the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. It's just a structureless atom. Like, we might in one application let p represent the claim "Carl is a good dog"; in that case it's very definitely true. But we might in another application let p represent the claim "Hexing the moon is a good idea"; in that case it's probably false. Or we might deal with applications that let p represent a particular electronic component that can be on or off, with the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} standing for these two states. So rather than prejudging anything, we consider every possible way of assigning values to the atomics.

Compounds, on the other hand, give us something to work with: their main connective. By choosing a scheme for determining the values of compounds based on the values of their components, we endow our connectives with a certain kind of meaning. In other words, we're using valuations to give our connectives meanings, while leaving our atomic sentences fully unspecified. Let's see how it works.

The scheme we'll start with is a very standard one. These valuations are often called `Boolean valuations', after the logicians George and Mary Everest Boole, although there are some small differences from the logical approach they developed.

George and Mary Everest Boole lived and wrote in England and Ireland in the 19th century, and Mary into the 20th as well. George is typically credited alone for the logical work he published, but I think it's worth noting that Mary was an author in her own right, their relationship was clearly an intellectual one, and George's most influential book, The Laws of Thought, was written during the time their relationship was beginning. So I think it's entirely likely that Mary had some influence on George's work, although how much and of what kind is something I could only speculate on.
Incidentally, here's Mary Everest Boole's advice for students encountering infinity:

"Angels, and square roots of negative quantities, and the other things that have no existence in three dimensions [she later explicitly includes infinity as an angel], do not come to us to gossip about themselves; or the place they came from; or where they are going to; or where we are going to in the far future. They are messengers from the As-Yet-Unknown; and come to tell us where we are to go next; and the shortest road to get there; and where we ought not to go just at present. When square root of minus one comes to you, behave reasonably about him. Treat him logically, exactly as if he were six or nine; only always remember to keep well in front of you the fact of your own ignorance. You may never find out any more about him than you know now; but if you treat him sensibly he will tell you plenty of truths about your xs and ys, and other unknown things".
From Mary Everest Boole, Philosophy and Fun of Algebra, 1909.
And here's her advice for me (and maybe also for you):

"You may some day become a teacher. If ever you are teaching a class how to set down a sum or an equation, say 'This is my way', or 'This is the way which I think you will find most convenient', or 'This is the way in which the Government Inspector requires you to do the sums at present, and therefore you must learn it'. But do not take in vain the names of great unseen powers to back up either your own limitations, or your own authority, or the Inspector's authority. Never say, or imply, 'Arithmetic requires you to do this; your sum will come wrong if you do it differently'. Remember that arithmetic requires nothing from you except absolute honesty and patient work. You get no blessing from the Unseen Powers of Number by slipshod statements used to make your own path easy."
From Mary Everest Boole, Philosophy and Fun of Algebra, 1909.

This last one, I think, is of some importance when it comes to logic. Unfortunately, the world has no shortage of people who are tempted to say "Logic tells us..." and then they fill in something they're trying to convince you of. Don't fall for it. Logic is about carefulness, orientation to detail, and discerning what you do and don't have support for. It does not require you to believe or disbelieve anything. (Rant over.)

To calculate the value that a compound sentence takes on a given valuation, we need to know two things: the main connective of the sentence, and the values that its components take on the valuation. For Boolean valuations, here's how the dependence goes. If we have a negation---that is, a sentence whose main connective is 
eg ---then that sentence takes the opposite value from its component. So if we have a valuation v such that v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, then v(
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and v(
eg 
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and v(
eg 
eg 
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and so on. I'll use a table like the following to record this:

This table tells you, for any value that a sentence A might have on a Boolean valuation, what value the sentence 
eg A must then have on that Boolean valuation. To use the table, if you know the value that a sentence A has on a Boolean valuation, find the row in the table that displays that value in the "A" column. Then you can read the value that that same row has in the "
eg A" column to see what value 
eg A must have. Boolean negation is simple enough that you probably won't need this table for very long, or maybe not at all; but we'll look at other options in a moment, so it's good to be able to read a table like this.

Why this table, rather than some other? That is, why have we chosen to set things up so that negations always take the opposite value from their components, rather than, say, always the same value, or always \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, or always \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}? (Note that those are the only four options, given our setup.) Those other options are all perfectly good options for a unary connective. The reason we've gone with the one we have is that we want \neg to have something to do with negation, with the idea of something not being the case. The truth-value-reversal idea we've gone with is one way to try to get at that. In English, if I say that the moon is made of green cheese, then what I've said is false; and so if I say it's not made of green cheese, then what I've said is true. The idea is to try to endow \neg with a meaning at least something like the meaning of "not"; and this particular table is one attempt to do that, probably the best choice we can make given our current limitations.

The same idea comes to the fore when we look at the remaining connectives in this language. In each case, we'll choose how to deal with them based on their intended interpretation: \land as something like "and"; \lor as something like "or"; \rightarrow as something like "implies" or "if...then"; and \bot and \top as, well, we'll get to them in a minute. We're working with a very simple and coarse-grained setup here, so the match might not be perfect. But thinking about how "and" and "or" and "if...then" work can get us some way along the path to seeing what we're doing with these connectives, just like thinking about "not" can help with understanding \neg . Here's how Boolean valuations deal with conjunctions: a conjunction A \land B gets the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} on a given valuation v exactly when both of its components A and B get value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} on the valuation v. Otherwise, the conjunction A \land B gets the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}.

Why deal with conjunction this way? Think, for example, about the claim "The moon is made of green cheese, and Carl is a good dog". Is this claim true or false? Well, "the moon is made of green cheese" is false, and "Carl is a good dog" is true. So if someone said to us "the moon is made of green cheese and Carl is a good dog", then what they've said is false: the falsity of the first part undermines the whole claim. They in effect said that both of these were true, and that's not so; so what they've said is false. This is why, for \land , we go with a setup that requires both components to be true on a valuation for the conjunction itself to be true on that valuation. Just as with negation, we can record this information in a table:

Since this table is for a binary connective rather than a unary one, we read it slightly differently from the table for negation. Let's say you want to use this table to calculate w(p \land q) on some valuation w where you know that w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} and w(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. Use the left component of the conjunction to choose a row of the table, by finding the appropriate value along the left edge. In this case, the left component is p, and w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, so you choose the bottom row of the table. Then, use the right component of the conjunction to choose a column of the table, by finding the appropriate value along the top edge. In this case, the right component is q, and w(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, so you choose the column headed by \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. By finding the entry where this row and column meet, you find the value your conjunction must take; in this case, this tells us that w(p \land q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. As with negation, you might find that you don't need the table for long, that it's enough to remember that both components must be true for the conjunction to be true. But as with negation, we're going to see more complex alternatives in a moment, so it's good to be able to read these tables.

The value of a negation or a conjunction, in a Boolean valuation, depends only on the values of its component(s). This is so even when the components are themselves compound sentences. So, for example, consider a valuation v where v(p) = v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}; you now know enough to calculate v(
eg (p \land 
eg q)). Do it step by step: first v(
eg q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then v(p \land 
eg q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and finally v(
eg (p \land 
eg q)) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. So once you know the Boolean tables for each connective, you will be able to calculate the value of any formula on any Boolean valuation, no matter how complex the formula is, so long as you know the values of the atomic formulas it contains.

For disjunction, Boolean valuations work the following way: they make a disjunction A \lor B true exactly when they make at least one of the components A and B true. To see why, think about "the moon is made of green cheese or Carl is a good dog". Is this true or false? This seems to be true: the truth of the second part is enough for the whole thing to be true. So for disjunction, we want to be sure to pick a table that respects this. The only way for a disjunction to be false, on this way of proceeding, is for both of its components to be false; any other combination makes the disjunction true.

Note in particular the connection between \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} \lor \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and logicians' weird habit 1. Whenever I say "or" in these notes, I mean to include the possibility of both sides of the "or" being true. And that's just what \lor does as well, on this scheme. This is a case of life imitating art; what's happening here is that uses of the English word "or" among communities of logicians are strongly influenced by experiences with the connective \lor .
Just as with conjunction, we can record this policy in a table:

This is the weird one. I won't make excuses; Boolean implication is not a very good approximation of "if...then". Don't lean too hard, then, on the understanding you already have of "if...then"; just roll with it. Here's the table:

That is, v(p \rightarrow q) comes out to \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} in all cases except the one case where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. It's relatively uncontroversial that p \rightarrow q should be false when p is true and q is false; if p really did imply q, and p is true, then q should be true as well, or at least so goes a pretty plausible line of reasoning. So the upper-right corner of the table is pretty strongly supported. But all of the other three entries can be (and have been) contested.

Note that, given the above table, we have v(p ightarrow q) = v(
eg p \lor q) for every Boolean valuation v, no matter what v(p) and v(q) are. (Later this week, we'll pick up a word for that: equivalence.) This might be the best way to see what's behind the table above: if p implies q, then we might think that either p isn't the case or else it is, and if it is, then since it implies q we can conclude that q would be as well. So if p ightarrow q is true, this might lead us to think that 
eg p \lor q should also be true. Conversely, suppose that 
eg p \lor q is true. Then what could we conclude if p were to be true? Well, since p and 
eg p can't both be true, it would imply that q must be the true component of 
eg p \lor q. That is, p would imply q. There's a lot you could quarrel with in these lines of reasoning, but I think something like them is probably the best motivation for this table for implication. Regardless, this is how Boolean implication works, and we'll work with it in this form.

There was, alas, an embarrassing moment, mainly from the 1950s to the 1990s or so, where a decent number of philosophers genuinely defended the idea that the Boolean implication is not just an approximation, but actually fully gives the truth conditions for English "if...then" statements. To see that this doesn't work, here's an old facetious proof of the existence of God:
It's false to say that if there's a God, then the prayers of the wicked will be answered. So, by the Boolean truth table, the antecedent of that "if...then" must be true; so there is a God.

Even theists should (and do) reject this line of reasoning; it's pretty obviously bad reasoning. Embarrassments like this for the idea that English conditionals are Boolean implications can be multiplied more or less endlessly; the view is as refuted as anything ever gets. But if you happen across certain philosophy from around that time period, you should be aware that many authors did in fact believe this, despite the obvious problems.

If you're interested in learning more about this, I recommend the book Relevant Logics and their Rivals, vol. 1. Note that this was written during the period in question! It's good fun, and it gives a particularly vitriolic (and particularly Australian) takedown of what was at the time practically an accepted orthodoxy.

Finally, verum and falsum. These don't really have great natural-language equivalents. But the idea is pretty simple: \top is meant to be like the truest possible thing, MAXIMUM TRUE; and \bot is meant to be like the falsest possible thing, MAXIMUM FALSE.

What do we need to specify about these? Well, like with any connective, we need to say how to determine a value for any sentence with \top or \bot as its main connective, given values for all its components. And: sentences with nullary connectives as their main connectives have no components; these sentences are just the connectives themselves.

So we need to give values for \top and for \bot to take on every Boolean valuation. Given their intended interpretations, the choice is clear. For any Boolean valuation v at all, we have v(\top ) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(\bot ) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}.
For uniformity, we can put these in "tables":

fr:title.text: Boolean valuations: putting it all together

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Boolean valuations: putting it all together

fr:mainmatter.html:p: For the language we're currently focused on, that's the whole story about Boolean valuations. With the above tables, if you know what values a valuation gives to all the atomic sentences, you can calculate what values that valuation gives to every sentence in our language. Of course, you usually won't know, of any particular valuation, what values it gives to all the atomic sentences, since that's infinite! But to calculate what a valuation v says about any particular sentence, it's enough to know what v says about just the atomic sentences that actually occur in that sentence; and this is always finite.

For example, consider any valuation w such that w(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and w(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and w(r) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, and suppose you want to know what w(p \land 
eg (q \lor r)) is. By looking at the table for disjunction, you can see that w(q \lor r) must be \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}; then by looking at the table for negation, you can see that w(
eg (q \lor r)) must be \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}; and finally by looking at the table for conjunction you can conclude that w(p \land 
eg (q \lor r)) is \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. By contrast, if we were thinking about a valuation v where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} and v(r) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, then we would find that v(p \land 
eg (q \lor r)) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. (Make sure you think that one through, to see how I got the answer!)
You now know enough to be able to do this for any sentence in our language. It's a good idea to get some practice in; this is the topic of an exercise type on https://binchicken.one.

Now I'm going to give you a slightly different way to think about what we've just gone over. This way is conceptually a bit more out there, but I think it makes it easier to do the calculations, and eventually even makes it easier to consider other possibilities. Don't worry too much if this section seems a bit much at first; but if it does, make sure you return here after you've had some more practice! What's here will in fact make things easier, once you grok it.

The conceptual move is this: we don't have to think about connectives as only acting within the language, forming compound sentences from their components. It can help to think of connectives as living a double life, acting on truth values directly. Rather than saying something like "for any sentence A and any valuation v, if v(A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, then v(\neg A) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}", we can say the same thing much more simply by just reporting that \neg \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. By saying this, we're not saying that \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} are sentences. They're not. They're still truth values, which are something else. What we're doing is extending our connectives so that they have the ability to act on truth values too. You can read all our earlier connective tables in this way. So, for example, you can consult the disjunction table to see that \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} \lor \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, or that \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} \lor \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}.

If you want to see this in action, you can check out https://negation.rocks/emu.html, which is another site I've made for logic practice. (You don't log in to this one, so it's not any part of your assessment; it can be a handy source of practice nonetheless, though.) If you head over there, you'll see that this is also the method I've used there to explain the correct answers for some exercises. Just as having a good notation for intermediate results can be useful in arriving at the right answer, it's also useful for explaining how the correct answer was arrived at, and hopefully it will be useful for you to be able to zero in on the missteps you make as you practice. (And of course you will make missteps! That's how learning works.)

So that's the practical benefit. The conceptual benefit is this: it lets us package up everything we know about Boolean valuations into something called a structure. A structure is a set of values, in this case \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, together with an account of how our connectives act on these values.
A structure for a language is a set of values, together with an account of how every connective in the language acts on those values.
And given a structure, we can say what it takes to be a valuation in that structure:

A valuation in a structure for a language is a function from sentences of the language to values in the structure, where the value of every compound sentence in the language is related to the values of its components in the way the structure indicates.
(Note that since any such function is a valuation, that means that valuations are not restricted at all when it comes to the values they assign to atomic sentences.)
This means that you can specify everything about how our Boolean valuations work just by putting down the tables we've already seen:

In giving a structure via tables like this, there's no need to separately specify the set of values, since you can read those off the edges of the tables. By packaging everything up into a structure in this way, we make it easy to change from one structure to another, to think about different kinds of valuations we might want to draw on, beyond just Boolean ones. In a moment, we'll look at a few other example structures, besides this Boolean one. But first, I want to look a bit at some uses of Boolean valuations.

fr:title.text: Uses of Boolean valuations: possible worlds

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Uses of Boolean valuations: possible worlds

fr:mainmatter.html:p: One way Boolean valuations get put to use is as surrogates for possible worlds. In certain areas of philosophy, talk of possible worlds is very common. This began, as far as I know, as an attempt to explain what we mean by saying that some eventuality is possible. To understand what we mean when we say that something is possible, some philosophers have proposed that this is the same as to say that it's true somewhere---just maybe somewhere else, at some possible world. And to understand what we mean when we say that something is necessary, some philosophers have proposed that this is the same as to say that it's true everywhere---that is, at every possible world. But even philosophers who don't accept this view as an analysis of possibility and necessity sometimes find it convenient to frame their views as involving possible worlds.

Exactly what these possible worlds are is a topic that much exercises metaphysicians, and for our purposes is best left to one side. Whatever possible worlds are, one thing they are often understood to do is to determine a truth value for every proposition, every claim about how things are. Moreover, there are meant to be many different possible worlds, determining these truth values in many different ways. These two features make them a lot like our valuations: every valuation fixes a truth value for every sentence in our language, and there are lots of different valuations, fixing these truth values in different ways.

For this reason, these Boolean valuations are often brought in for study as surrogates for possible worlds. This is particularly common among thinkers who actually believe, what we have only supposed, that every sentence really is either true or false, and no sentence is both---and moreover, who believe that this not only happens to be the case, but is necessary. That view fits well with the two-valued setting Boolean valuations provide.

These valuations are only surrogates, though and not the worlds themselves. This is because possible worlds are meant to settle actual claims. For example, Ellie likes to dance is true in the actual world, but not in some other possible worlds; but nothing in our toy logical language has to represent Ellie likes to dance. We just have ps and qs and such, and their compounds, not any real claims. Still, the analogy is close enough that it has sustained a lot of philosophical work on possible worlds.

Another way Boolean valuations get used is in understanding certain kinds of electrical circuits. In particular, Boolean valuations are of most use for considering circuits where every component has only one of two states: on or off. Consider, for example, a light installed in the ceiling that can be controlled from either of two switches. Each switch has two possible states: on or off. And the light has two possible states: on or off. These states are related to each other in a somewhat complex way, and we can use our language, and Boolean valuations, to understand it.

Let's understand the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} now, not as truth values at all, but instead as the states on and off, respectively; and let's understand our components---the two switches and the light---as sentences in our language. Call one switch p and the other switch q. Just as we have valuations that give us every possible combination of values for p and q, so too we can run around the room and set up every possible combination of states of the two switches.

What sentence should we associate with the light? Well, how should the light respond to the switches? We want to set things up so that, no matter what state things are currently in, and no matter which switch we're nearest to, we can always change the state of the light by changing the nearest switch. A diagram might help here. We have four possible states of the switches, which I'll call v_{1}--v_{4}, and we can move between these states as shown here:

We can think of these states as Boolean valuations on a language with the two atomic sentences p and q. For example, we'd have v_{2}(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, since switch p is on in state v_{2}; and v_{2}(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, since switch q is off there.

We want to wire up a light now so that, whichever one of these states we happen to be in, flipping p will change whether the light is on or off, and so that, whichever one of these states we happen to be in, flipping q will also change whether the light is on or off. That is, we want the light either to be on in states v_{1}, v_{4} and off in states v_{2}, v_{3}, or vice versa. Can we build a sentence, then, using the atomic sentences p, q and our connectives \land , \lor , \neg , that works like this? If we can, the structure of such a sentence will tell us how to wire up the light. (That is, it will tell us how to wire up the light if we have electrical components that correspond to the connectives \land , \lor , and \neg ! Such components are standardly called AND, OR, and NOT gates, respectively.)
Can you find such a sentence?

In fact, there are infinitely many sentences that would work here (although there are also infinitely many that don't work!). Consider, for example, the sentence (p \lor q) \land \neg (p \land q). If you do your Boolean calculations out, you will see that this sentence takes the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} on valuations v_{2} and v_{3}, and takes the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} on valuations v_{1} and v_{4}; that makes it an example of what we're looking for. But there are infinitely many other sentences that work equally well here; it's great if you came up with a different one!

If you've ever made even a small redstone device in Minecraft, you've almost certainly made use of Boolean valuations in this way; redstone torches are the component corresponding to \neg , and just running two lines of redstone together corresponds to \lor .

There is no simple block in Minecraft that calculates \land ; instead, there are various ways to combine 
eg and \lor that produce the same results. For example, consider the two sentences p \land q and 
eg (
eg p \lor 
eg q). By checking all the possible values p and q can take, you can see that these two compound sentences have the same value as each other in every valuation. So even if you don't have a component that calculates \land directly, so long as you have components for 
eg and \lor , you can get the job done.

However, Minecraft redstone, like real wiring, is way more complex than this! Only some Minecraft devices are on/off the way Boolean valuations are. Basically anything involving a comparator, you'd want to be thinking in terms of a sixteen-valued logic, not a two-valued logic. (For an introduction to this sixteen-valued logic, you can see this talk by the logician Roy Cook.) Concerns about timing and spatial arrangement, which are central to redstone (and real!) wiring, are also not captured by Boolean valuations.

And the flash memory in your phone is almost certainly what's known as NAND flash memory, because it's based on a circuit that calculates something known as NAND. NAND is another binary connective, one we won't be taking much of a look at this semester. But it too can be built from what we do have: p NAND q always has the same value as \neg (p \land q). Indeed, this is why it's called "NAND"; the name comes from "not-and".

You might have heard that computers, at their most basic level, deal only with zeroes and ones. Taken literally, this is just plain not true. But you're now in a position to understand the thing that is true that explains why people say this.

An electrical conductor can carry varying amounts of electric potential (this is the thing that's measured in volts). In most computers, however, the conductors that carry and hold information are not treated in a way that varies smoothly; they are kept either at a high voltage or at a low voltage. That's what computers deal with at a basic level: high voltage and low voltage.

It's conventional to represent that high voltage with the number 1 and the low voltage with the number 0 for human readability. So it's not that computers use lots of zeroes and ones; it's that we use lots of zeroes and ones to represent differing voltage levels. Computers use these differing voltage levels.
But why zero and one? Why not the actual voltage measurements, or the symbols L and H, or anything else? The answer goes back (again) to the Booles.

In The Laws of Thought, George Boole draws a connection between conjunction between properties and multiplication on numbers. But he notices a disanalogy: for most numbers n, we do not have n \times n = n. However, for any property P, being P and P is the same (so Boole says) as being P. This might seem to threaten the analogy between conjunction and multiplication---but Boole pulls a tricky turn.

Note that we do have n \times n = n in the special cases where n is either zero or one. So Boole says that properties behave not like just any numbers, but like the numbers zero and one in particular, because these are the numbers that are their own squares. As far as I can tell, this is the origin of the zero/one notation that we still see today.

Now, let's turn to a different structure. In the last section, we thought about two truth values: \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. As part of that, we supposed that every claim was either true or false, and not both. But this might be too restrictive. Lots of people think that there are claims that are neither true nor false, or claims that are both true and false.
For example, think about certain claims about the future, like "In 2036 the ALP will no longer exist". Here's a view someone might hold about sentences like this:

"For such a claim to be true, there would have to be something right now that guarantees its truth. But there isn't any such guarantee. Sure, the ALP might go out of existence in the nearish future, as its urban base goes more and more to the Greens; but this trend might reverse, or the ALP might adapt to it and find other ways to survive. Similarly, for such a claim to be false, there would have to be something right now that guarantees its falsity. But there isn't any such guarantee. Sure, the ALP might manage to survive until 2036, but they also might continue to stand for the rapidly-dissolving status quo and so fall apart. Or climate change might completely upend the Australian political system. So the claim isn't true, and it isn't false."

Now, there's lots to disagree with in the above passage. Maybe you think that the ALP is guaranteed to survive the next 15 years, or alternately that it's already doomed. If you think either of those things, though, you can probably just change the example: pick something about the future that you think isn't guaranteed. (Of course, that only works if you think there's something that isn't guaranteed! So there's another place you might disagree.)

But even if you think the ALP's future isn't guaranteed either way, you still might resist the claim that something has to be guaranteed to be true/false in order to be true/false. You might think instead that, even though there's not now any guarantee which one it is, still either the ALP will be around in 2036 or not, and that's all it takes for the claim to be either true or false. You don't have to think that we can now know the truth value of a sentence to think that it has one, and similarly you don't have to think that something is determined in order to think that it's true.

Whether you agree or disagree with the point of view represented in the offset passage, though, we can use logic to try to understand the point of view. To do this, we go along with the passage. The values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} alone aren't enough to do this. According to the passage, there is another option: some sentences are neither true nor false. We can use the symbol \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} for this third option.

For a different kind of example, picture a colour that's right on the border of what you'd call "blue". Pick a colour where, if someone asked you whether it was blue yes or no, you wouldn't be sure how to answer. Thinking about this colour, consider the claim "The colour is blue". Here's a view someone might hold about claims like this:

"There are lots of different places we can reasonably draw the line between what's blue and what's not blue. For a claim like this to be true, it's enough that there is some way to draw the line that makes the claim come out true. Since the colour at least isn't obviously not blue, there is a reasonable way to draw the line that includes the colour. So the claim is true. At the same time, for a claim like this to be false, it's enough that there is some way to draw the line that makes the claim come out false. Since the colour isn't obviously blue, there is a reasonable way to draw the line that excludes the colour. So the claim is also false. The claim, then, is both true and false."

Again, there's lots to disagree with in this passage. Maybe you think that there's no colour that could be reasonably classified as blue and also reasonably classified as not blue. Or you might think that it isn't enough for the claim to be true that the colour could be classified as blue, or not enough for the claim to be false that the colour could be classified as not blue. But whether you agree or disagree with the view in this passage, we can use logic to try to understand it. Again, to do so we go along with the passage. Now, we should think that even the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} alone aren't enough; we need some value for claims that are both true and false. Let's use a new value \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} for this purpose.

So now we've got four truth values to think about: \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}. Of these, \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} are as before; we use these for things that are true (without also being false) and things that are false (without also being true), respectively. The new value \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} is for things that are neither true nor false, and the new value \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} is for things that are both true and false.

Let's make this into a structure for our language. To do so, we need to say how our connectives are meant to work on the four values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. For this, there are tons of options; too many to realistically survey them all. For example, for negation alone we need to make four choices (what values are 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, and 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}?), and for each of these there are four possible answers. That gives 4^4 = 256 possibilities, and that's just for negation alone. Each binary connective requires sixteen choices, giving 65,536 possible tables to choose from for each of \land and \lor .
Again, though, we're just going to look at one particular structure from this mess of options. The idea will be to imitate, as well as we can, the way things worked in the Boolean structure.

Here's an example of what I mean: in the Boolean structure, we have 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} and 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, so we'll bring those over into our four-valued structure as well. What about 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}? Well, recall that \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} is meant to represent being both true and false. We've decided that a negation should be true when its component is false, and false when its component is true. So if its component is both true and false, the negation itself should also be both: false because its component is true, and true because its component is false. So we have 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}. And similarly for 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}: this can't be false because \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} isn't true, and it can't be true because \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} isn't false, so it must not be either. That is, 
eg \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}. Different structures might go different ways here; it's not like this is forced on us or anything. But it's at least a reasonable place to work from, and it's what we'll go with. This gives us the table:

For \land , we can adopt the same strategy. Our guiding principles will be this: a conjunction is true exactly when both of its components are true, and false exactly when at least one of its components is false. That gives us the following table:

Think, for example, about \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} \land \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}. Is it true? Is it false? Well, its left component is true, and its right component is both true and false, so both of its components are true; the overall conjunction must be true as well. Also, since its right component is both true and false, at least one component is false, so the overall conjunction must also be false. That means the overall conjunction is both true and false, and so has the value \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, as the table says. All the other entries in this table can be arrived at in the same way.

This does mean that we've got to distinguish between being true and taking the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. On this kind of structure, there are two different ways to be true: one represented by \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and the other by \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}. Just like being both tall and happy is a way of being tall, being both true and false is a way of being true. Similarly, we've got to distinguish between being false and taking the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. On this kind of structure, there are two different ways to be false: one represented by \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} and the other by \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}. Just like being both tall and happy is a way of being happy, being both true and false is a way of being false.

And we can do the same for disjunction, with these guiding principles: a disjunction is true exactly when at least one of its components is true, and false exactly when both of its components are false. That gives us the following table:

For implication, let's stick to what we noticed for Boolean valuations: that v(p \rightarrow q) = v(\neg p \lor q), no matter what values v(p) and v(q) are. Let's require that here as well; it gives the following table for implication. (Compare the implication table to the disjunction table; note that the difference is just in swapping the first and last rows with each other. Why is that?)

Finally, let v(\bot ) always be \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}---still MAXIMUM FALSE, and now not at all true either; and let v(\top ) always be \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}---MAXIMUM TRUE. So now we've got a full structure for our language: four truth values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, and a full specification of how our connectives operate on those truth values. I'll call this structure the Dunn-Belnap structure, since it is often associated with the logicians J. Michael Dunn and Nuel Belnap.

Belnap and Dunn were US logicians; Dunn was Belnap's PhD student. My own logical lineage (and so now yours as well) traces back to them; Dunn taught Gary Hardegree, who taught Jc Beall, who taught me.

With the Dunn-Belnap structure in hand, we have a new kind of valuation available: Dunn-Belnap valuations. These valuations work just like our Boolean valuations did, except they make use of the Dunn-Belnap structure instead of the Boolean structure.

That is, we again allow total freedom for atomic sentences: every possible way of assigning one of the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} to each atomic sentence is a valuation. And we again use this to make it so that each valuation determines a value for every sentence in the language, by following along with the Dunn-Belnap structure.

So, for example, consider a valuation v where v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and v(r) = \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}. What is v(p \land (\neg q \lor r))? Pause for a moment and try to answer this question before you read on; you have enough information to do it.

Ok, here we go. To calculate the value that the valuation v assigns to p \land (\neg q \lor r), I'll first replace the atomic sentences with the values v assigns to them. So we're looking at \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} \land (\neg \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} \lor \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}). With that done, we just go step by step, following the tables:
\mathsf {\mathbf {T}} \land (\neg \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} \lor \mathsf {\mathbf {N}})
\mathsf {\mathbf {T}} \land (\mathsf {\mathbf {B}} \lor \mathsf {\mathbf {N}})
\mathsf {\mathbf {T}} \land \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}
\mathsf {\mathbf {T}} So v(p \land (\neg q \lor r)) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}. Evaluating sentences in the Dunn-Belnap structure like this is an exercise type over at https://binchicken.one.

Just like Boolean valuations are sometimes used as surrogates for possible worlds, so too are Dunn-Belnap valuations. The difference between them, in this use, amounts to a difference in what counts as possible. For example, in every Boolean valuation v, we have v(p \land 
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}; it doesn't matter whether v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} or v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. So using Boolean valuations for possible worlds fits well with a view where contradictions like p \land 
eg p---sentences that say that something both is and isn't the case---are impossible. On the other hand, in Dunn-Belnap valuations, when v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, then v(p \land 
eg p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} as well. That is, there are Dunn-Belnap valuations in which contradictions can count as true (and also false). So using Dunn-Belnap valuations for possible worlds fits well with a view where contradictions like p \land 
eg p are possibly true. That is, we can use different logical tools to try to understand different views about what's possible.

This gives one example of a very common phenomenon to see in uses of logic. Once we've set up a particular logical approach to something, we get firm results that follow within that approach. In the example, if we choose to use Boolean valuations for possible worlds, we get the result that all contradictions are impossible. But that's not because "logic itself" forces those results on us (whatever that would mean). It's just because we set up the particular approach we did. If we had set up a different approach, we could have gotten different results. In the example, if we instead use Dunn-Belnap valuations for possible worlds, we get the result that contradictions are possible. So the study of logic alone is probably not going to teach us much about, say, whether contradictions are or are not possible. Logic is rich and varied, and can be used to explore both options. If we want to reach firm conclusions about whether contradictions are or are not possible, those conclusions are going to have to come from somewhere else. Logic will be happy either way.

That doesn't mean, though, that people who think that contradictions are always impossible can have no use for Dunn-Belnap valuations. It just means that they'd use them in a different way. Indeed, Belnap himself was among the people who think that contradictions are always impossible, and the application I'm about to lay out is due to him.

For example, imagine that you only know what people tell you, that people only tell you about atomic sentences, and that the people who tell you things only use the Boolean values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. (That's a pretty significant idealization, given that you're a human, but it's perhaps not too far off the situation certain databases find themselves in.) Since different people might tell you different things, this gives you four possibilities for each atomic sentence: either you've just been told that it's true; or you've been told both that it's true and that it's false; or you've just been told that it's false; or you haven't been told anything at all. We can use the Dunn-Belnap values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}, respectively, to record these states. So a Dunn-Belnap valuation can now be seen as giving a record of what you've been told about what atomic sentence.

By calculating the values of compound sentences on this valuation, we can see what you've been implicitly told about these compounds. For example, if you've been told both that p is true and that it's false, and you've been told just that q is false, then we can represent your information state with valuation v such that v(p) = \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} and v(q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}. What have you been implicitly told about p \land q? Well, you've been implicitly told that it's false, since any conjunction with at least one false component is false, and you've been told that both components are false. And you haven't been implicitly told that it's true, since it takes two true components for a conjunction to be true, and nobody's told you that q is true. So you've implicitly been told that p \land q is false, and not that it's true. And indeed, v(p \land q) = \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}, following the Dunn-Belnap structure. In this way, we can see Dunn-Belnap valuations as representing certain kinds of epistemic states: states that can be conflicted (through the value \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}), or that can remain neutral (through the value \mathsf {\mathbf {N}}), as well as simply believing something to be true or false.
I'll pause here to introduce a useful idea: the idea of sentences being equivalent to each other in a structure. (Think "equi-valent" like "equal-valued".)
Sentences A and B are equivalent in a structure iff, for every valuation v in that structure, v(A) = v(B).

Note two things: first, we need to have a structure in mind to say when two sentences are equivalent, and we might get different results by looking at different structures. Second, once we have a structure in mind, equivalence looks at every valuation for that structure, at every possible way of assigning values to atomic formulas.

For example, p and 
eg 
eg p are equivalent in the Boolean structure: no matter what Boolean valuation v we look at, v(p) = v(
eg 
eg p). But p and q are not equivalent in the Boolean structure. Yes, there are some Boolean valuations that give the same value to p as to q, but that doesn't matter. Since there is at least one Boolean valuation w with w(p) 
eq w(q), that means that p and q are not equivalent in the Boolean structure.

We've set up our implication in the Dunn-Belnap structure so that p \rightarrow q comes out equivalent in this structure to \neg p \vee q. That was inspired by the Boolean structure, where these same two sentences are also equivalent. And it will continue into our next structure as well. However, this is not required; structures can set up their connectives any which way. If you want to play with some structures where p \rightarrow q and \neg p \vee q are not equivalent, you can check out some of the structures at https://negation.rocks/emu.html.

As you might have noticed, in presenting Dunn-Belnap valuations, I started from the Dunn-Belnap structure. That's how I'll continue to present different kinds of structures and valuations: by presenting the structure first. That determines what the valuations are: the different ways of assigning the values in the structure to the atomic sentences, extended to all sentences by following what the structure says about the connectives.
Let's look now at the following structure, known as the strong Kleene structure, named after the logician Stephen Kleene:

Kleene (pronounced "CLAY-nee") was a 20th-century US logician. His son would later say, of the pronunciation CLAY-nee, "As far as I am aware this pronunciation is incorrect in all known languages. I believe that this novel pronunciation was invented by my father". (See https://foldoc.org/Stephen+Kleene.)
This is a very widely used and studied structure, and it's one we'll use a lot this semester.

Indeed, this structure is so widely used and studied that there's not really any single fixed interpretation we can give to the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}. Sometimes people think of it as "neither true nor false", sometimes as "both true and false", sometimes as "half true", sometimes as "an unknown truth value", and sometimes in other ways. But however it is interpreted, the structure is the same. For now, pay attention just to the structure itself.

fr:title.text: Looking at a structure without interpreting it

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Looking at a structure without interpreting it

fr:mainmatter.html:p: Even without any interpretation at all for the value \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, we can still see how the strong Kleene structure works, and we can build up strong Kleene valuations using this structure in just the way you would expect. (Again, you have exercises on this at https://binchicken.one.) The same is true of the values \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}}; you don't need to think of \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} as connecting to truth, or of \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} as connecting to falsity, to be able to see how the strong Kleene structure works in full.

Indeed, you don't need to think of \mathsf {\mathbf {T}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {*}}, \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} as truth values at all; let's just call them "values". All you need, in order to be able to use the structure, is to know how connectives act on these values. That information is contained entirely in the above tables. So you don't need to interpret this structure at all to be able to use it.

The same is true of all our structures. Although I've tried to help you get the feel for the Boolean structure and the Dunn-Belnap structure by having you think about truth and falsity, the structures themselves don't depend on this way of interpreting them. All the structures really are is some collection of values and a way to calculate on them using the connectives.

This is why we're able to use these structures for purposes other than thinking about truth. Recall our electrical interpretation of the Boolean structure. A switch that's on isn't true, and a switch that's off isn't false; they're just on or off. But we used the value \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} to represent being on and the value \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} to represent being off. This required divorcing \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} from their initial interpretation as truth values.

Similarly when we think about the epistemic interpretation of the Dunn-Belnap structure. You might have been told both that Carl is a good dog and that Carl's not a good dog, and we can use the value \mathsf {\mathbf {B}} to keep track of that. But that doesn't mean that "Carl is a good dog" really is both true and false. (In fact, it's not false at all!) So in this interpretation, we're using the values as records of reports, again not as truth values. It's possible to interpret these structures in many different ways because the structures themselves don't depend on any particular interpretation; they're fully abstract.

So if \mathsf {\mathbf {T}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {F}} don't have to have anything to do with truth and falsity, why are they called "\mathsf {\mathbf {T}}" and "\mathsf {\mathbf {F}}"? Here's the real answer: well, we had to call them something, and the suggestion of truth and falsity carried by the names "\mathsf {\mathbf {T}}" and "\mathsf {\mathbf {F}}" can be useful for building your intuition, as long as you don't get stuck on the idea. The same goes for the values \mathsf {\mathbf {N}} and \mathsf {\mathbf {B}}, and the names "\mathsf {\mathbf {N}}" and "\mathsf {\mathbf {B}}" we're using for them. Once you're familiar with these structures, the names for the values don't matter at all. We could easily use different names.

For example, sometimes people will present the Boolean structure using the names "1" and "0" for its values, rather than "\mathsf {\mathbf {T}}" and "\mathsf {\mathbf {F}}", as I've done here. But it's still the same structure, so long as all the connectives behave the same way. For example, \neg 1 = 0, and 1 \lor 0 = 1, and so on. We're just calling the values something different; as long as they act the same the difference doesn't matter.

In thinking about all of our structures, then, it can be helpful to open up a part of your mind that lets you think about them without any particular interpretation in view, and even without any particular names for the values in view, since these names are often linked to particular interpretations. As much as you can, try to just think about the values themselves and how the connectives operate on them, separate from any ideas about truth, or electricity, or possible worlds.

It's by proceeding in such an abstract way, separate from all applications, that we can get away with doing the work just once. It can then be applied over and over, wherever we want it. The logician Rósza Péter put the point this way (emphasis added):

"Mathematics is really a language, a queer language which speaks entirely in symbols. The above formula is only a symbol, and means nothing by itself; everybody can substitute into it [their] own experiences. For one it might mean the counting of the diagonals of a polygon, for another the counting of the number of possibilities for choosing the leading pair among [their] pupils. The writing down of a formula is an expression of our joy that we can answer all these questions by means of one argument."
From Rósza Péter, Playing With Infinity, 1955

Rósza Péter was a Hungarian logician in the mid-20th century. Her work was particularly important in the development of an area of logic known as "recursion theory". (See the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy article here for an introduction.)
"The writing down of a formula is an expression of our joy". I think Péter has hit the nail on the head.
